entries.title: Former Bears assistant — and nearly head coach — Dave McGinnis dies at 74
entries.summary: <p>Dave McGinnis, the longtime Bears assistant coach whose botched head-coaching hire led matriarch Virginia McCaskey to replace her own son as team president, died Monday at 74.</p><p>McGinnis was the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a>’ linebackers coach from 1986-95, mentoring, among others, Mike Singletary, Otis Wilson and Wilber Marshall. He became the Cardinals’ defensive coordinator in 1996 and was the Bears’ first choice to replace head coach Dave Wannstedt when he was fired in January 1999.</p><p>After the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2022/1/18/22889969/bears-mccaskey-track-record-odds-they-get-coach-gm-right-bill-polian-george-mccaskey" target="_blank" >Bears</a> interviewed five candidates, they sent out a press release and scheduled a news conference to welcome McGinnis as the Bears’ 12<sup>th</sup> head coach — only McGinnis had never agreed to a contract. The news conference was delayed, then canceled. Disturbed by the unusual process and upset by contract terms that he considered below the industry standard, McGinnis left Halas Hall and never accepted the Bears’ job. The team apologized.</p><p>The Bears hired Dick Jauron the next day. Three weeks later, Virginia McCaskey decided to replace Michael McCaskey as president with vice president of operations Ted Phillips, who negotiated contracts for the team. It marked the first time in Bears history that someone outside of the Halas and McCaskey families held the president title.</p><p>Michael McCaskey became chairman of the Bears board of directors, where he remained until 2011.</p><p>In 2018, McGinnis told the Talk of Fame Network that the Bears flew him in for a one-day interview and asked if he’d stay another day. He agreed and then was “announced as the head coach before I had even been offered the job,” he said. He said that when he drove to Halas Hall to find out what was going on, he heard a WGN-720 AM news report that said he’d be introduced as head coach at 11 a.m.</p><p>McGinnis became an NFL head coach eventually, taking over the Cardinals on an interim basis in 2000 and going 17-40 over four seasons.</p><p>He became the Titans’ linebackers coach in 2003 and stayed until 2011 before following head coach Jeff Fisher to St. Louis. He served as the assistant head coach at both spots.</p><p>He spent the nine years as a color analyst for the Titans’ radio broadcasts. He was admitted to a Nashville hospital March 4 and was visited by friends and former players in recent days, according to the Titans.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Cubs left-hander Matthew Boyd nearing return from strained left biceps
entries.summary: <p>PHILADELPHIA — Opening Day starter <b>Matthew Boyd</b> is getting closer to a return from a strained left biceps.</p><p>The Cubs put Boyd on the 15-day injured list April 6 (retroactive to April 3) but didn’t seem too concerned about his injury. Boyd said that it was taking him longer to bounce back from starts and that he was dealing with soreness in the biceps. He said that if it hadn’t been early in the season, he likely would have pitched through it.</p><p>Manager <b>Craig Counsell</b> said Monday that Boyd threw a bullpen session Sunday and that ‘‘everything went great.’’ He will go to Triple-A Iowa for a rehab start and is lined up to return next week at home against the Phillies.</p><p>The Cubs need Boyd at his best as they navigate this season without promising right-hander <b>Cade Horton</b>, who is going to miss the rest of 2026 after having surgery on his pitching elbow.</p><p>Boyd’s last start was a 10-strikeout masterpiece against the Angels. His ascent into an All-Star pitcher last season was one of the driving forces behind the Cubs making the playoffs.</p><h3>Bullpen puzzle</h3><p>Figuring out the bullpen is a constant challenge for Counsell and president of baseball operations <b>Jed Hoyer</b>. Bullpens are fickle and change from season to season. Only <b>Daniel Palencia</b> and <b>Caleb Thielbar</b> returned from the bullpen that ended last season.</p><p>The constant flux has become normal for the Cubs, but they already have hit a couple of roadblocks with their relievers after injuries to <b>Phil Maton</b> (tendinitis in right knee) and <b>Hunter Harvey</b> (inflammation in right triceps). Maton was the rare reliever to receive a multiyear contract under Hoyer’s tenure, and though the Cubs knew Harvey had a lengthy injury history, they thought signing him was worth the risk.</p><p>Now that they’re down two key bullpen pieces — and with <b>Colin Rea</b> having shifted to the starting rotation in the short term — the Cubs recently have received admirable efforts from <b>Riley Martin</b> and <b>Ethan Roberts</b>.</p><p>‘‘Riley’s made a big move with his opportunity, and we’ve got to make sure we get him some rest,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘But he’s pitching great. He’s pitching great, and he’s pitching with some confidence for somebody that’s only pitched three big-league innings.’’</p><p>Both pitchers’ preparation has stood out. Entering the game Monday against the Phillies, neither had yielded a run.</p><p>‘‘They both have stuff that really stands out and can be really tough on hitters,’’ Thielbar said. ‘‘It’s really just about going out there and executing.’’</p><h3>Kelly’s hot start</h3><p>Entering the game Monday, catcher <b>Carson Kelly</b> was batting .308 with an .810 OPS. He delivered the walk-off single Sunday against the Pirates and has been one of the Cubs’ more consistent offensive performers while others have started the season slowly.</p><p>‘‘He’s taken a lot of the opposite-field hits all over the park,’’ Counsell said.</p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Cubs not good enough in any phase of game in blowout loss to Phillies
entries.summary: <p>PHILADELPHIA — The Cubs were outclassed in their 13-7 loss Monday to the Phillies at Citizens Bank Park.</p><p>They failed to execute in all three phases of the game. The pitching was subpar, the offense was listless until the game was well out of reach and the defense wasn’t nearly sharp enough as the Phillies capitalized on every Cubs miscue.</p><p>The Phillies already led 4-2 in the fifth — on the strength of two home runs by former Cub Kyle Schwarber — before loading the bases on a walk and back-to-back singles. Brandon Marsh followed with a two-run double to make it 6-2, but the Phillies didn’t stop there. They kept applying pressure and extending the lead against Cubs starter Javier Assad.</p><p>Alec Bohm knocked in a run with a sacrifice fly, Bryson Stott had an RBI single and J.T. Realmuto delivered another run-scoring single before Cubs manager Craig Counsell pulled the plug on Assad, who allowed career highs of nine runs and 11 hits in 4⅓ innings in his second start of the season.</p><p>‘‘I just don’t think he got his sinker going and didn’t execute with his fastball,’’ Counsell said afterward.</p><p>The Phillies routinely hit the ball hard, running up the score and stringing together productive at-bats. The game got so out of hand that both teams started subbing guys out in the seventh. The Phillies scored in six of the eight innings in which they batted.</p><p>The Cubs were still in the game, trailing only 4-2, when they squandered a prime scoring opportunity in the fifth. They had runners on first and second with one out and Ian Happ, who leads the team in homers with four, at the plate. Instead of keeping the line moving, however, Happ grounded into a double play to end the threat.</p><p>Phillies left-hander Cristopher Sanchez used his sinker and changeup to keep the Cubs’ hitters off-balance all night.</p><p>‘‘We gave ourselves some chances but weren’t ultimately able to come through with the big hit,’’ shortstop Dansby Swanson said.</p><p>The Phillies routinely came through with the big hit. They were 8-for-14 with runners in scoring position; the Cubs were 4-for-16.</p><p>Sanchez is one of the best pitchers in the majors and entered the game having allowed only three runs in his first three starts. He wasn’t at his sharpest against the Cubs, but they let him off the hook.</p><p>When the Cubs did get hits against him — six in six innings — five were singles. The only runs they scored against him came on a two-run homer by Swanson in the fourth. Sanchez struck out eight and walked three.</p><p>Despite the blowout loss, Swanson remained confident in the Cubs’ ability to put up runs. He said they are putting themselves in position but aren’t taking advantage of the scoring chances. Through 16 games, the Cubs have yet to produce a consistent stretch of good baseball.</p><p>‘‘Early in the year, we can all get caught [up] when it’s a small sample size,’’ Swanson said. ‘‘One hit can completely change what that sample size and data point looks like; we just haven’t consistently gotten it. But the more you keep putting yourself in position to be successful, the better off you’re going to be. Our group does that consistently, and I’ve got no worries that it’s going to happen.’’</p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Injured plover returns to Montrose Beach: 'Pippin is a trooper'
entries.summary: <p>A familiar friend returned Monday to the shores of Montrose Beach, according to the Chicago Park District and a group of birdwatchers.</p><p>Pippin, a Great Lakes Piping plover, has returned to the Chicago lakefront for his third year.</p><p>Last summer, Pippin left the lakefront with an injury to his foot. The Chicago Piping Plovers, a group of birdwatchers which began <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/04/11/piping-plovers-montrose-beach" target="_blank" >when the birds </a>were first spotted, said many feared the worst after his injury last year.</p><p>Pippin returned this week with a missing foot and subtle limp. Otherwise, the Chicago Park District said he is in good health. </p><p>“Pippin is a trooper,” said Irene Tostado, a spokesperson for the Chicago Park District, in a statement.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <blockquote class="bluesky-embed" data-bluesky-uri="at://did:plc:k33a6y5zoxkzmhobrj4hczls/app.bsky.feed.post/3mjftdw6thc2l" data-bluesky-cid="bafyreiazjgywih7zsdctgtdrr6n3sjsmdvtjdys2h5iaehtjzro5rshzmy"><p lang="en">Welcome home, Pippin! 🤍🟢 Our beloved Pippin has returned. Many will remember he departed last summer with something tangled around his foot. We feared the worst. Pippin has returned to us with his right foot missing and a limp but otherwise appears well. 📸:Liam Francis Shanley</p>— <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/did:plc:k33a6y5zoxkzmhobrj4hczls?ref_src=embed">Chicago Piping Plovers (@chicagopiping.bsky.social)</a> <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/did:plc:k33a6y5zoxkzmhobrj4hczls/post/3mjftdw6thc2l?ref_src=embed">2026-04-13T21:40:11.030Z</a></blockquote><script async src="https://embed.bsky.app/static/embed.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div></div><p>An endangered species, Pippin joins other <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/outdoors/2024/07/27/against-all-odds-piping-plovers-keep-making-history-in-illinois" target="_blank" >Piping plovers at Montrose Beach</a>. The Chicago Piping Plovers also reported Monday that one of the other plovers, Imani, had returned to the lakefront. The 2,000 acres of nature are managed by the park system for their biodiversity and wildlife habitat.</p><p>“The presence of this endangered species is a testament to the importance of protecting our environment and wildlife through continuous conservation and sustainability efforts,” Tostado said in the statement. </p>

Social occasions might be unpredictable with cancellations or surprise invitations.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you feel optimistic, upbeat and happy, which is why you're full of big ideas and enthusiastic suggestions. Meanwhile, your home routine might be interrupted. Small appliances might break down, a minor breakage could occur or surprise company might appear at your door. Stock the fridge.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Respect your moneymaking ideas because you might boost your income today. Meanwhile, pay attention to everything you say and do because this is a mildly accident-prone day. Your daily routine might be interrupted. On the upside, you might have a brilliant idea.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the moon in your sign, dancing with lucky Jupiter, ensures that your relations with others will be warm and friendly. Expect to attract happy, positive people to you. However, keep an eye on your money and possessions. Something unexpected might happen. (You might also have a worthwhile moneymaking idea.)</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actor and film director Peter Capaldi (1958) shares your birthday today. You're a curious social butterfly who is in touch with your environment. You're intellectual and physically restless with strong emotions. This is a year of new beginnings, adventures and major changes. Keep your eyes open for new opportunities. What you do now will bring great benefits in the future. </p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Blackhawks' effort levels crash to zero in blowout loss to Sabres
entries.summary: <p>After a couple weeks of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>' effort meter flickering up and down, it crashed to near zero in their penultimate game Monday.</p><p>Mental and emotional engagement were visibly lacking in a 5-1 loss to the Sabres, who are heading to the playoffs for the first time in 15 years. </p><p>Nothing summed up the Hawks’ futility more than a line change after a penalty kill that they bungled to an almost comical degree, allowing <b>Tage Thompson</b> to bury the Sabres’ fourth goal.</p><p>Later, the Hawks’ backcheck after a turnover by <b>Wyatt Kaiser</b> in the offensive zone was arguably even lazier than the line change.</p><p>An announced crowd of 17,056 — which appeared several thousand short of that figure — missed no opportunities in the third period to boo the Hawks, to the point that in-arena host <b>Jon Hansen</b> joked about it during a commercial-break gimmick.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">5-1 Sabres. Not sure where the backcheck was: <a href="https://t.co/bZ8FjdDBW1">pic.twitter.com/bZ8FjdDBW1</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2043887849041265146?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 14, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>If it didn’t happen in the 81st game of a going-nowhere season, this kind of performance would justify a reckoning about the fitness of the Hawks’ roster and coaching staff. Considering it was, it instead can — and will — be forgotten soon.</p><p>Nevertheless, the atrocity that is this season-ending homestand — during which the Hawks already have been outscored 17-6 ahead of their finale Wednesday against the Sharks — is destroying optimism at a brisk pace entering the offseason.</p><p>“It’s a little unfortunate these last couple games have gone this way because, honestly, we’ve done tons of good things to build this in the right direction, and unfortunately, this sours your taste,” coach <b>Jeff Blashill</b> said.</p><p>“When you play great this time of year, it can fool you. When you play poorly this time of year, it can fool you.”</p><h3><b>Against odds</b></h3><p>The Hawks will finish in the bottom three of the NHL for a fourth consecutive season, becoming the first team since the 2001 Lightning to “achieve” that distinction.</p><p>Their 31st-place finish means they’ll enter the draft lottery on May 5 with a guarantee of a top-four pick. They’ll also have the second-best odds to receive a top-two pick.</p><p>Those odds still aren’t very high: 13.5% of getting the No. 1 pick and 14.1% of getting No. 2. It’s more likely they end up with the third or fourth.</p><h3><b>Toothless Nazar</b></h3><p>All things considered, <b>Frank Nazar </b>avoided the worst-case scenario when another puck struck his face Saturday in the Hawks’ loss to the Blues. But his two top front teeth did not.</p><p>The puck knocked them out, leaving him with a gap in his smile Monday and some swelling in his lips. He started the game wearing a cage, then switched to a bubble.</p><p>“I still got it pretty good, but it could be a lot worse, so I’m pretty thankful,” Nazar said, struggling to pronounce words correctly with the missing teeth. </p><p>“If I’m lazier, I’ve got a little bit of a lisp. But if I’m more firm, you can kind of understand me.”</p><h3><b>Unsatisfied Greene</b></h3><p><b>Ryan Greene</b> reached the 10-goal mark in his rookie season with his goal Saturday, then tallied his 11th goal shorthanded Monday.</p><p>The quantity of chances he has missed still "doesn't sit well" with him, though. He analyzed his overall performance this season as "responsible" but unspectacular. </p><p>That aligns with Blashill's recent comments about Greene being a player he trusts to not let things get "bat-[expletive] crazy."</p><p>"You kind of know what he's going to do," Blashill said.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Child left alone on Garfield Park street
entries.summary: <p>Someone left a little girl alone outside on a West Side street Monday evening, according to Chicago police. </p><p>The girl, believed to be between 2 to 3 years old, was found around 6:50 p.m. in the 4300 block of West Van Buren Street after a female left her and fled in a black SUV, police said. </p><p>The girl has brown hair with braids and was wearing a pink coat with maroon and yellow strips, a pink Mickey Mouse sweater, pink Hello Kitty shoes, pink<br>Adidas book bag containing diapers and an iPad with a pink case, officials said.</p><p>Anyone with information on the child should contact Area 4 detectives at 312-746-8251.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 2 cops among 8 hurt in Back of the Yards crash
entries.summary: <p>Two Chicago police officers were among eight injured in a traffic collision early Tuesday in the Back of the Yards on the South Side. </p><p>Around 4:15 a.m., the officers were in a marked squad car in the 5100 block of South Hermitage Avenue when a black SUV ran a stop sign and the two vehicles collided, Chicago police said. </p><p>The officers were taken to area hospitals where they were in fair condition, police said. </p><p>The driver of the SUV and five passengers were also hospitalized with non-life threatening injuries, officials said. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Dear Abby: Worker ready to bail on first post-college job
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I'm struggling with what to do about my first job out of college. I've been here for four months, and while I expected a learning curve, I didn't expect the environment to feel so hostile. My boss yells at me across the office for small, easily fixable mistakes. The latest incident involved her slamming her hands on the table several times and shouting, "What are you talking about?" while I was trying to clarify a question. I couldn't even get my words out.</p><p>I'm in the second round of interviews for another job with a different company, and I'm torn about what to do. My parents think I should stick it out to avoid being seen as a job hopper. But I feel anxious going into work every day. This environment is eroding my confidence.</p><p>Furthermore, I will be moving to a new town with my fiance next year, so I'm wondering if it's smarter to stay for another several months or take the new job (which will be remote, if I get it) even though I'm worried I might not like that one either.</p><p>Am I too sensitive? Should I leave a job this quickly, or push through until my move? How do I make the right decision when I feel guilty no matter what I choose? — <i>CONFLICTED IN NEBRASKA</i></p><p><b>DEAR CONFLICTED:</b> This is your first job. Because you will be moving next year, rather than jump to a new one, stick to the one you have because it will look better on your resume. Your boss may be difficult, but she also may be dealing with stresses about which you know nothing. It might benefit you to talk with the other employees about how they cope when something like this happens. They may be able to offer some helpful suggestions.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I host dinners for most holidays and birthdays for my relatives and friends. I have a relative and a friend who have enjoyable and shared interests, but every time they come, they announce that they do not cook — as if it is a badge of honor. I'm beginning to resent the comment after spending hours shopping, planning and preparing the meal for everyone. When they do offer to contribute, they bring a tasteless pie or an item from a discount bargain store.</p><p>Both are seniors, and I realize they are not going to change. One offered to help me clean up, then criticized me for "wasting" water while I hand-washed and rinsed my china dishes. (They do offer compliments and praise for the special event.) I just wish they'd offer, or make, one item that is a special contribution. I will continue to temper my resentment over their comments, but I am finding it increasingly difficult. Help! — <i>STEAMED IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR STEAMED:</b> I have one question for you: You know these folks are set in their ways and unlikely to change. Why do you continue to invite them if this bothers you so much? Because your meal is planned in its entirety, you shouldn't need an extra store-bought dessert. How about assigning them some other task, such as bringing flowers or something to nibble on while waiting for the meal to be served. (Nuts, anyone?)</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For everything you need to know about wedding planning, order "How to Have a Lovely Wedding." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Wedding Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: After a flood, mold sticks around and can make people sick
entries.summary: <p>Dorothy Rosenthal couldn’t believe her eyes.</p><p>Four feet of water overwhelmed her basement in West Garfield Park, destroying everything in a storage area, laundry room and an office in July 2023.</p><p>A load of memories — from Mother’s Day cards from her two kids, to old toys, and other sentimental items were ruined. Her computer was destroyed. So was her washer and dryer and her boiler system.</p><p>That was just the beginning.</p><p>The storm “left mold, big black patches of mold,” she says. “Big bubbles, black bubbles on the wall.”</p><p>Almost three years later, Rosenthal finally has had the mold removed from her home, persistent black gunk that lined every wall of her basement.</p><p>It wasn’t just unsightly, the mold aggravated her asthma, a condition she’s been struggling with for about 20 years.</p><p>“It got to the point that … I saved all the washing for Saturday. And once I would wash, two days afterwards I wouldn’t be able to taste or smell,” Rosenthal says.</p><p>“I can now look forward to washing without being sick for two days after and I am grateful for that,” she says.</p><p>The relief came when a West Side nonprofit was allowed to administer a relatively small pool of city dollars to pay for the cleanup and remediation.</p><p>Rosenthal had to live with mold because she couldn’t afford to pay a private contractor thousands of dollars to get rid of it.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d70000" name="image-d70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" alt="A contractor removes all the mold from a basement that was flooded." 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/91fa9f3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F9b%2F1ec3221649e0b7065aec4a9d5706%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghealthimpacts-260414-05-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/af79974/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe7%2F9b%2F1ec3221649e0b7065aec4a9d5706%2Fdelugedbc-floodinghealthimpacts-260414-05-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p> Almost three years after flooding damaged her home and left mold in her basement, Dorothy Rosenthal finally has had the mold removed.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3></h3><h3>Climate change brings more rain, more flooding, more mold</h3><h3></h3><p>Rosenthal and her neighbors in Chicago and across Illinois are confronting the effects of more<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/10/flooding-chicago-climate-change-deep-tunnel-mold-metropolitan-water-reclamation-district-soaked" target="_blank" ><u> intense flooding</u></a> and more severe weather. Climate change is contributing to thunderstorms that hit harder and more frequently, like ones that caused flooding in 70,000 West Side and suburban basements in summer 2023.</p><p>Those storms are also leaving behind serious health threats, including mold, which can make people sick. Tiny mold particles can enter the respiratory system, causing fatigue, rashes and other complications. People with compromised immune systems, respiratory illness and the very old or young can be affected more seriously.</p><p>“People will have totally different reactions,” says Dr. Sheetal Rao, an internal medicine doctor at UI Health. “It all has to do with your exposure and vulnerability.”</p><p>In one particularly serious condition known as hypersensitivity pneumonitis, patients have an allergic reaction to mold that can lead to scarring of the lungs, making it difficult to breathe, says Rush University System for Health pulmonologist Dr. Samuel Fox.</p><p>The problem with tackling mold as a public health threat is that there are many types of mold, too.</p><p>“The science is very complicated,” adds Dr. Arturo Casadevall, a professor of medicine at the Johns Hopkins School of Medicine. “There are a zillion black mold species. So, it’s not easy to sort out one species.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-380000" name="image-380000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9edb3b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6179x3468+0+326/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2Fd0%2Fffb1e3974d1f937490914d1dbb0e%2Fflooding-072623-14.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/46922e9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6179x3468+0+326/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2Fd0%2Fffb1e3974d1f937490914d1dbb0e%2Fflooding-072623-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="FLOODING-072623_14.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/46922e9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6179x3468+0+326/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2Fd0%2Fffb1e3974d1f937490914d1dbb0e%2Fflooding-072623-14.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8e94aa2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6179x3468+0+326/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2Fd0%2Fffb1e3974d1f937490914d1dbb0e%2Fflooding-072623-14.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/46922e9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6179x3468+0+326/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fff%2Fd0%2Fffb1e3974d1f937490914d1dbb0e%2Fflooding-072623-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mold covers the basement walls of a home in Chicago’s Austin neighborhood. Experts say mold can make people sick.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>A silent public health threat</h3><p>Unfortunately, the city health department offers no help to flood victims.</p><p>“We don’t handle mold,” the Chicago Department of Public Health said in a statement.</p><p>The department does acknowledge that “mold exposure can result in respiratory issues like sneezing, coughing or wheezing, especially for people who suffer from asthma or allergies.”</p><p>Neither the city nor the state health departments have numbers on cases of mold-induced illness.</p><p>It’s a public health hazard that’s not officially recognized, other than in public housing. Federal housing authorities provide guidelines for Chicago Housing Authority and other agencies to protect residents from mold.</p><p>Doctors may determine their patients have developed health issues from the exposure.</p><p>Mold can also be stubbornly hard to remove because it embeds in drywall and other surfaces.</p><p>“Three years after, and the mold grows,” says Rubye Bailey, who lives in Austin. She also just had work done to remove mold from her finished basement, which flooded in 2023. “Mold took over.”</p><p>One West Side politician notes work needs to be done to prevent this kind of flooding, including improving infrastructure to manage the stormwater.</p><p>“More is needed to mitigate these growing environmental challenges and reduce resulting potential health concerns related to basement mold, debris and dirty water,” says Ald. Emma Mitts (37th). “This is an important public health issue.”</p><p>In 2023, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2023/7/25/23807114/austin-flood-damage-mayor-brandon-johnson-fema" target="_blank" ><u>Mayor Brandon Johnson walked</u></a> through Austin, his neighborhood, as well as other West Side communities, vowing to help residents. He told them government officials were doing everything they could.</p><p>Now, Johnson says help is still on the way. His administration is figuring out how to spend more than $400 million from federal housing officials related to the floods.</p><p>"While the pace of change can always be faster,” the mayor said in a statement, “my administration is committed to making sure these dollars get to the people who need them most as efficiently as possible."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.title: Same-sex couple households increase over 70% in Chicago since 2005, new Census data shows
entries.summary: <p>Kim Ricardo and her wife are representative of a growth of same-sex couple households, which new U.S. Census Bureau data shows has nearly doubled nationally in the last 19 years. In Chicago, it's up 72% in that time.</p><p>Ricardo, 48, met her wife, Katerine, while traveling in Argentina 10 years ago.</p><p>“She's actually Cuban, but was living in Argentina,” Ricardo said. “We met dancing at a tango milonga.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-bf0000" name="html-embed-module-bf0000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28506475"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28506475/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p></p><p>They live in River North and have created a robust community around them. They’ll soon celebrate nine years of marriage, and they welcomed a baby girl in September.</p><p>Ricardo is Filipino-American, a law professor at University of Illinois Chicago and a vice chair of the Chicago Dance Makers Forum. She moved to Chicago in 2008 and recalled a conversation she had with her realtor as they looked at homes in Andersonville.</p><p>“She said this is like 'girlstown,' and I was like, ‘I don't think so,’" Ricardo recalled “I didn't see evidence of that.”</p><p>Having lived in Chicago for the last 18 years she said she does see a big change both in society and in the law.</p><p>“Chicago is really a good way to map changes in society,” Ricardo said. “When I first moved to Chicago and I joined the law school, there was no civil union, there was no domestic partnership.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.census.gov/library/stories/2026/04/same-sex-couples.html" target="_blank" ><u>The new U.S. Census Bureau data shows same-sex couple households</u></a> in the U.S. went up 78% since 2005, with most of the growth happening in the last 10 years. The survey uses <a class="Link" href="https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/acs.html" target="_blank" ><u>American Community Survey</u></a> one-year estimates for all years between 2005 and 2024. It showed about 1.4 million same-sex couple households in the United States in 2024.</p><p>According to the data, within Chicago's defined community areas, the two locations with the highest percentage of same-sex couple households are the area including Uptown, Edgewater and Rogers Park followed by the Lake View and Lincoln Park area.</p><p>While nationally female same-sex couple households outnumber male same-sex couple households, the numbers show more male same-sex couple households in Chicago, and more female in the suburbs and rest of the state.</p><p>In Chicago same-sex couples make up 1.7% of all couples. It's about 0.75% in the rest of Illinois.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-d70000" name="html-embed-module-d70000"></a><div class="flourish-embed flourish-chart" data-src="visualisation/28505732"><script src="https://public.flourish.studio/resources/embed.js"></script><noscript><img src="https://public.flourish.studio/visualisation/28505732/thumbnail" width="100%" alt="visualization" /></noscript></div> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Changes in how the Census Bureau tracks same-sex couple households</h3><p>The rise in the count of same-sex couple households nationwide could reflect a more accurate representation of couples living together across the country. But experts point to various factors that can contribute to the jump in the data — from the evolution of Census survey methods to the legalization of same-sex marriage.</p><p>In <a class="Link" href="https://www.census.gov/library/publications/1993/dec/cqc-02.html" target="_blank" ><u>1990 the U.S. Census survey began asking respondents about their sex</u></a>, their age and their relationship to the household head, said Christine Percheski, an associate professor of sociology at the Institute for Policy Research, Northwestern University. They included a category for unmarried partners, so respondents could identify same sex partners, if they were the same sex partner of the household head.</p><p>“They improved some of the wording and categories, and they've changed some of them over time,” Percheski said. “Between 1990 and 2000 we saw that category almost double in that first 10 years but remember, that's before same-sex marriage is legal in any of the states.”</p><p>Percheski points to widespread discrimination against same-sex couples during that time. It was also before states recognized civil unions for same-sex couples, and before many of the protections for people who identify as LGBTQ+.</p><p>“There were some ways that we could see some of this in some Census surveys and some Census data, but there were reasons that people weren't publicly identifying or marking themselves as an unmarried partner," Percheski said.</p><p>In Chicago, between 2014 and 2024, the number of same-sex couples identifying as married grew by 95% compared to an increase of 15% for opposite sex-couples.</p><p>This aligns with what was happening on the policy level in Illinois and nationwide. Illinois’ Religious Freedom and Marriage Fairness Act <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/publicacts/view/098-0597" target="_blank" ><u>took effect in June of 2014</u></a><u>,</u> allowing same-sex marriage and giving same-sex couples legal rights and protections. Then in 2015, <a class="Link" href="https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/576/644/" target="_blank" ><u>U.S. Supreme Court case </u><i><u>Obergefell v. Hodges</u></i></a> legalized same-sex marriage nationwide. The ruling required all states to license and recognize same-sex marriages, overturning all existing state bans.</p><p>Percheski believes the newly released numbers could still not be showing an accurate picture of the status of households in the U.S.</p><p>“There are also concerns about the safety of LGBTQ folks and people in same sex couples,” Percheski said. “It's always a bit of a dance because we want to fix the technical errors leading to the wrong numbers, but we also have to acknowledge that some people might not want to be reporting on government surveys because of concerns about their safety or discrimination.”</p><h3>Do same-sex couples feel represented in new Census numbers?</h3><p>Ricardo said she does struggle with the two conflicting options. On one hand she wants to see families like hers reflected on government surveys and forms but she worries about how politicians will use that information.</p><p>“I think that trans folks are subject to some of the most sort of hateful violence, and our leaders in public office are not willing to do anything to try to remedy that,” Ricardo said. “In fact, I think they try to capitalize on some of the division that's created in order to just score political points.”</p><p>Ricardo said former President Barack Obama's administration tried to create a more diverse and fluid language when it came to the Census and how the government talked about families and identities like hers, but now she sees a backlash.</p><p>“We saw in Trump’s first administration and in the second Trump administration an effort to kind of silence or eliminate that vocabulary. What it threatens to do is to erase the legibility of families like mine from our social text,” Ricardo said. “Taking away that language and making it suspect, taking away just the simple thing of my ‘pronouns are’ is really disheartening but it has the effect of erasing people and communities.”</p><p>During President Donald Trump's first term, his administration blocked efforts to get questions about sexual orientation and gender identity onto a Census Bureau survey. Former President Joe Biden later renewed that process to quell advocates' calls to add questions that would identify people’s sexual orientation, gender identity and household. The Trump administration has since paused those efforts.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-cb0000" name="image-cb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/52b6241/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Ffe%2F9015240245808411e73ba0bc3795%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-023.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/84e2ac1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Ffe%2F9015240245808411e73ba0bc3795%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-023.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/643c849/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Ffe%2F9015240245808411e73ba0bc3795%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-023.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Phyllis Johnson stands under a magnolia tree in her Roseland front yard Monday on Chicago's South Side. In 1994, Johnson co-founded "Women of All Cultures Together (WACT)," an organization for queer women to build community. | Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/643c849/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Ffe%2F9015240245808411e73ba0bc3795%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-023.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3c5dce1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Ffe%2F9015240245808411e73ba0bc3795%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-023.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/643c849/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F64%2Ffe%2F9015240245808411e73ba0bc3795%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-023.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Phyllis Johnson stands under a magnolia tree in her Roseland front yard Monday on Chicago’s South Side. In 1994, Johnson co-founded Women of All Cultures Together, an organization for queer women to build community. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Phyllis Johnson, 76, and her wife, Barbra Smith, first met at a potluck brunch group. Johnson co-created the brunch group Women of All Cultures Together in the early 1990s so lesbian women could meet and create community.</p><p>After knowing each other a few years, Johnson and Smith began a romantic relationship and moved in together in 2008 and eventually married. Johnson said if they had the opportunity to identify their same-sex household status on any census survey they would. </p><p>“I was a late bloomer, I came out in my 40s,” Johnson said.</p><p>When Johnson and Smith began dating, they each lived in their own home but they would often talk about retiring somewhere warm. One day a mutual friend asked them if they were ever going to live together before retirement.</p><p>“It just never crossed our minds that I'd move in with her, which would have been perfect,” Johnson recalled.</p><p>When they finally did move in together in Roseland on the South Side, their neighbors asked questions.</p><p>“‘OK, are you cousins? Are you sisters? What are you?’" Johnson said. “Gay never came up, lesbian never came up. That was not the vocabulary. In the early 2000s there was still a ton of social pressure. But that remarkably changed by the time you get to 2020.”</p><p>Still, Johnson, whose wife died in 2015, does not see many same-sex couples in their neighborhood. She said there are still very conservative pockets among the Black community.</p><p>“I know a lot of people that are estranged from their families because of their sexual identity and their gender identity,” Johnson said. “I think in general, society is much more open, but I think there's some hotbeds of social conservatism at least in the Black community.”</p><div class="Enhancement" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9f14d43/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7980x4479+0+422/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2Fc7%2Ff4b3eb4742df9a681d8f7bab6980%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-009.jpg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/db80746/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7980x4479+0+422/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2Fc7%2Ff4b3eb4742df9a681d8f7bab6980%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Phyllis Johnson holds a picture of her late partner, Barbra Smith, in her Roseland home." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/db80746/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7980x4479+0+422/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2Fc7%2Ff4b3eb4742df9a681d8f7bab6980%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-009.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/85a0c29/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7980x4479+0+422/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2Fc7%2Ff4b3eb4742df9a681d8f7bab6980%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-009.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/db80746/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7980x4479+0+422/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd9%2Fc7%2Ff4b3eb4742df9a681d8f7bab6980%2Fsamesexcouples-260414-009.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Phyllis Johnson holds a picture of her late partner, Barbra Smith, in her Roseland home. Johnson and Smith met through Women of All Cultures Together, an organization Johnson co-founded in 1994 for queer women to build community.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>She said knowing and seeing the option to identify as a same-sex couple household on the U.S. Census survey does make her feel seen but that could change.</p><p>“I know people, my peer groups that go back in the closet when they require nursing home services,” Johnson said. “I wouldn’t be surprised if the number didn't go backwards after the Trump administration because he's going to be in office until '28 and it's right on top of the census.”</p><p>Percheski said the data from the survey is a start but how the data is gathered is important. She said the survey is not designed to study same-sex couples across the country.</p><p>“It's designed to gather data that is used by lots of different parties, including local government officials, who are trying to figure out how many schools and local government, local employers and people designing employment,” Percheski said. “We can study same sex couple households in this data but to get at the nuance [of same-sex couples], we might have to add a lot more questions.”</p><p>Ricardo says they have noticed more same-sex couples cohabiting in their River North neighborhood, especially more diverse couples.</p><p>“I think that that's a huge change. You just see a bigger group of young people, racially diverse, ethnically diverse, and I think that's really nice to see,” Ricardo said. “I come to Chicago from living in lots of different places. It's nice to see Asian American queers out.”</p><p>Data from Chicago indicates a significant rise in mixed-race, same-sex couple households from 9% in 2005 to 36% in 2024.</p><p>“I think it is a result of our changing societal norms and also our laws protecting same sex couples and queer people rights in general,” Ricardo said.</p>
entries.author: Araceli Gómez-Aldana | WBEZ

entries.title: Developer behind McKinley Park's Pershing Terminal project receives $250,000 grant
entries.summary: <p>IBT Group was one of two Chicago developers to recently receive a $250,000 grant from the Federal Home Loan Bank of Chicago's Community First Developer Program, whose goal is to help emerging real estate developers and support community-focused housing solutions.</p><p>IBT will use the funding to support paid internships and a senior fellow who will help lead housing efforts on the developer’s next project: Pershing Terminal in McKinley Park.</p><p>The roughly $179 million investment will transform the former Quartermaster Depot, 1769 W. Pershing Road, into a mix of workforce housing, life sciences lab and office space, retail and green space. The depot was built for the U.S. Army in 1918.</p><p>The project will also help advance the city’s goal of spurring more economic development and employment opportunities in McKinley Park.</p><p>“If you go through those streets, most of those houses, they're well kept, family owned for a long time but haven't seen material investment,” IBT Group founder and President Gary Pachucki said. “This will be a big investment, which will create jobs, [and] have an impact on their whole neighborhood. I think it's going to help bring life and bring new housing, and then hopefully, that will spur other housing as well.”</p><p>IBT was selected by the city to redevelop the site in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/9/11/23865037/winner-selected-pershing-road-project-reinvent-mckinley-park" >September 2023</a>, following Requests for Proposals in 2022 for redeveloping the city-owned site. Other redevelopment proposals at the time included a movie studio and mixed-use business space.</p><p>The former warehouse is a “complicated building," Pachucki said. The 571,476-square-foot, six-story building has massive floor plates, which Pachucki plans to break up by carving out a center courtyard.</p><p>“By taking out the center third of the building, you effectively create two apartment towers, and they have this big atrium,” Pachucki said. “Those buildings are really solid — they're all concrete — so it's going to be a massive demolition project, but then what you'll have is this really cool space.”</p><p>Plans call for about 172 apartments, but Pachucki said they're trying to include 200. The units will be below market rate, with 20% of them affordable, according to Pachucki.</p><p>The units, an average of 1,000 square feet, are also slightly larger than the typical apartment because of the intended demographic. IBT is targeting scientists, tech workers and other life sciences professionals who want to live where they work. They're often in their late 20s to 30s and want space for their partner or children, Pachucki said.</p><p>The building's rooftop will include two amenity decks and green space. </p><p>The eastern portion of the 6.5-acre site, which has a one-story city maintenance facility, will be redeveloped into free-standing retail and parking.</p><p>Pershing Terminal builds off IBT’s The Terminal in Humboldt Park. That $70 million project is Humboldt Park’s first horizontal science and technology campus, offering affordable spaces for startups and growth-stage companies. Pachucki hopes that vision can be replicated in McKinley Park.</p><p>Pachucki thinks Pershing Terminal will help fill a void that startups are facing: a lack of affordable office space.</p><p>IBT is raising funds for the project, and once it's fully capitalized, Pershing Terminal should take about two years to complete.</p><p>In addition to IBT, Beauty for Ashes Developers was also awarded $250,000. Beauty for Ashes, a women- and minority-owned firm founded in 2019, will use the funding to expand its fellowship and internship programs.</p><p>Amalgamated Bank of Chicago, a member of FHLBank of Chicago, helped the two developers secure the grants, which were announced last month.</p><p>Kimberly Kam, senior vice president and sales director of union and commercial services at Amalgamated Bank of Chicago, said the funding is key for the selected developers. The award will allow them to keep housing affordable by helping to cover staffing costs, as opposed to paying out of pocket and raising project costs.</p><p>It also expands opportunities for women and people of color to get their start in the commercial real estate industry, she said.</p><p>“The biggest part of this grant is really just creating and paving a path for future commercial developers,” Kam said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/9/11/23865037/winner-selected-pershing-road-project-reinvent-mckinley-park" >Winner picked for Pershing Road project; plans would reinvent an industrial colossus</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Fatal Washington Heights fire which killed boy, 10, caused by electrical issues, police say
entries.summary: <p>A fire that killed 10-year-old London Woodard in Washington Heights Monday was caused by electrical issues, according to Chicago police. </p><p>The fire erupted just before 3:30 a.m. in the main level of his home in the 10400 block of South Green Street. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/boy-10-killed-in-washington-heights-fire-dreamed-of-playing-in-nba-to-take-care-of-older-brother" target="_blank" >London Woodard</a> was found in the house and was taken to Roseland Hospital where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>An 18-year-old was also pulled out of the home and was taken to Loyola University Medical Center in critical condition, police said.</p><p>Preliminary investigation determined the cause of the fire was caused by electrical issues, according to a police report, which said the blaze was "non-criminal."</p><p>“He was a joy to be around,” recalled London's uncle Robert Harris Jr.. “He was always laughing and playing.”<br></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Boy, 17, fatally hit by CTA train in Rogers Park
entries.summary: <p>A 17-year-old boy was fatally hit by a CTA train Tuesday morning in the Rogers Park neighborhood, authorities said. </p><p>Police were called about 8:15 a.m. to the Jarvis station, 1523 W. Jarvis Ave., for the boy who was hit by an oncoming train, Chicago police said, adding he was pronounced dead on the scene. </p><p>Chicago fire department paramedics also responded to the victim, who was on the tracks, according to a spokesperson. Another person was taken to an area hospital in good condition, according to the spokesperson, who had no further information immediately.</p><p>In a social media post, Ald. Maria Hadden (49th), said the person hit by the train had died by suicide.</p><p>A teenager has died by suicide this morning at the Jarvis L Station, Hadden's post said. "This is a tragedy that hurts our whole community. Our condolences to their family and friends," Hadden said in the post. </p><p>The situation has caused delays and a service disruption along the Red Line in the area, according to the CTA, which said no trains were running between the Howard and Thorndale stops. </p><p>An autopsy is scheduled and Area 3 detectives are investigating. <br></p>

JB Pritzker and his gubernatorial rival Darren Bailey, are defending Pope Leo XIV — as President Donald Trump faces backlash for calling the head of the Catholic church "terrible for foreign policy" and posting a since-deleted AI-generated image of the president as Jesus.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Leo last week spoke out after Trump threatened to wipe out "a whole civilization" in Iran, urging people to call for their political leaders to "work for peace and reject war always." On Monday, he told the Associated Press: "I'm not afraid of the Trump administration or of speaking out loudly about the message of the Gospel, which is what the Church works for."</p><p><b>'Sleeping giant'</b>: "I think that he’s going to rally people of faith to say, 'Thank God he’s standing up,' and that we stand with him," said the Rev. Michael Pfleger of St. Sabina Church. "I think Trump unconsciously is waking up a sleeping giant ... I think [the Pope is] going to be a very strong voice, and he’s not going away. He’s not going anywhere."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-h/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>How do you feel about recent statements from President Trump and Pope Leo related to the Iran war? Please explain.</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply to this email</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-k/" target="_blank" >After Illinois floods, mold sticks around and can make people sick</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-u/" target="_blank" >Brett Chase</a></h5><p><b>Worsening flooding</b>: Residents in Chicago and across Illinois are confronting the effects of more<a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-o/" target="_blank" > intense flooding</a> and more severe weather. Climate change is contributing to thunderstorms that hit harder and more frequently, like ones that caused flooding in 70,000 West Side and suburban basements in summer 2023.</p><p><b>Health threats</b>: Those storms are also leaving behind serious health threats, including mold, which can make people sick. Tiny mold particles can enter the respiratory system, causing fatigue, rashes and other complications. People with compromised immune systems or respiratory illness, and the very old or young, can be affected more seriously.</p><p><b>One woman's story</b>: Dorothy Rosenthal couldn’t believe her eyes. Four feet of water overwhelmed her basement in West Garfield Park, destroying everything in a storage area, laundry room and office in July 2023. The storm "left mold, big black patches of mold," she says. "Big bubbles, black bubbles on the wall." It wasn’t just unsightly; the mold aggravated her asthma, a condition with which she’s been struggling for 20 years.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-b/" target="_blank" >Record number of CPS students earn biliteracy seals</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-p/" target="_blank" >Emmanuel Camarillo</a></h5><p><b>Dual linguists</b>: Last year, more than 3,500 Chicago Public Schools students earned the seal of biliteracy, a state award that recognizes students who show they can read, write and speak at a high level in English and another language. In 2015, the first year that awards were presented to graduates, only 91 students received the recognition.</p><p><b>How we got here</b>: Educators and experts say that growth is due to a combination file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-yd/" target="_blank" >State House speaker backs millionaires tax, but Democrats split over details</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-yh/" target="_blank" >Mawa Iqbal</a></h5><p><b>How to tax the rich?</b>: Illinois House Speaker Emanuel "Chris" Welch is endorsing a proposed 3% tax on those making more than $1 million annually, but there is a split among House Democrats on how much of the potential $4.5 billion yearly windfall should fund property tax relief. On the table are two competing measures that would put the question of a so-called millionaires tax on the November ballot as an amendment to the Illinois constitution.</p><p><b>The options</b>: One proposed amendment offered by state Rep. La Shawn Ford, D-Chicago, would allocate all of the funds toward property tax relief in the form of $1,500 rebates per property owner. A competing plan by state Rep. Natalie Manley, D-Joliet, would allocate half of the millionaires tax’s proceeds to public schools and the rest toward property tax relief.</p><p><b>Deadline nears</b>: Neither bill has been put to a House vote as lawmakers barrel toward a May 3 deadline to get constitutional amendment proposals passed in order to be on the Nov. 3 general election ballot.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-yk/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-jl/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-7352d8f4-3804-11f1-aae2-75157d866204"><li><b>AI and education</b>: Alpha School, opening this fall in Chicago, says its AI-driven model can help students learn core academics in just two hours a day. >Mitchell Armentrout</a></h5><p>The Indianola Independent Advocate had the scoop on Duncan Davitt’s call-up to the White Sox.</p><p>One owner of the central Iowa newspaper got a tip straight from the source Wednesday, hardly believed it — and soon broke down in tears, the 26-year-old pitcher reported.</p><p>Of course, publisher Amy Duncan couldn’t be closer to the story. Davitt’s her son, and he's a sports columnist for the family-owned newspaper.</p><p>"My mom cried. She thought I was joking at first, which, I don’t know what that’s about," Davitt, the budding write-hander — er, right-hander — joked from the other side of the notebook Thursday in the visitors’ clubhouse at the Kansas City Royals' stadium.</p><p>Royals games were a regular destination for Davitt while growing up some three hours away near Indianola, a city of about 16,000 in the Des Moines area. He covers prep sports there in the offseason.</p><p>But Davitt himself was the story Thursday, arriving in Kansas City, Missouri, as a big-leaguer awaiting his major-league debut, which he made toward the end of Friday's game.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-ty/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: How has <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiilljl-l-tt/" target="_blank" >Chicago’s worsening flooding</a> affected you?</p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"Ruined my 92-year-old mother's basement, and no relief or repair was available. entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: 2 men killed hours apart on South and West Sides
entries.summary: <p>Two men were fatally shot about two hours apart Monday night in the East Garfield Park and Englewood neighborhoods. </p><p>Around 11:40 p.m., a 26-year-old man was found unresponsive with a gunshot wound to the left shoulder in the 7400 block of South Morgan Street, Chicago police said. </p><p>He was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>About two hours earlier, a 27-year-old man was standing in front of a residential building in the 3600 block of West Congress Parkway when three gunmen approached and opened fire, officials said. </p><p>The man was shot multiple times in the body and was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital where he died, police said. </p><p>Their names have not been made public. </p><p>Area detectives were investigating both homicides. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Trump feuding with the pope? I thought he WAS the pope
entries.summary: <p>By now you know what happened.</p><p>President Donald Trump, up to his neck in the Iran quagmire and thus perhaps more angry than usual, lashed out at Pope Leo XIV for doing what popes do: urging peace, while pointing out that the efforts of the Trump administration to paint their war as the Ninth Crusade were not Christianity in its highest form.</p><p>"Pope Leo is WEAK ON CRIME, and terrible for Foreign Policy," Trump began, in a long tirade posted on his Truth Social network.</p><p>No doubt you've read all or part of it.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-2b0000" name="module-2b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>So the "what" being established, I want to explore an aspect that is being lost in the noise and thunder:</p><p>Why?</p><p>Why would a struggling and isolated leader, having failed to lure his erstwhile allies into saving his butt in Iran, start tongue-lashing the pope? A beloved figure internationally, but particularly in the United States, and especially in his hometown, Chicago. A pope who, remember, wasn't doing anything beyond normal pope stuff — promoting harmony, encouraging brotherhood. That's like blasting Mr. Rogers for being neighborly.</p><p>You would think that anyone with half a grip, his back against the wall, closing the Strait of Hormuz himself because Iran won't open them, would not pick this battle. It's like a man in a blazing room setting fire to one sofa that isn't burning.</p><p>Again: why?</p><p>If the answer isn't crystal-clear — and really, it should be, by now — here's a clue.</p><p>Last May, when Leo was named pope, Trump distributed an AI picture of himself, Donald Trump, in the garments of the Vicar of Christ. Because — and forgive me, this is obvious, but so much so that it gets overlooked — it's all about him. He is the subject of all sentences, the cynosure of all eyes, and anyone else — <i>anyone</i> else — who isn't actively groveling before him is an insult and a threat. There is no Congress. No courts. No law. No pope. <i>He</i> is the pope. Donald Trump, <i>pontifex maximus.</i></p><p> To me, Donald Trump is a morality tale about the futility of ego. He suffers from a grandiosity so bottomless that being immensely rich, the president of the United States, adored by millions, the golden spoon stirring the world pot for the past decade, are not enough. Nothing is ever enough. He is King Midas, breaking his teeth on gilt apples, starving in a room full of food.</p><p>That's the only way any of this makes sense. It explains his every action.</p><p>Put yourself in his place. Now you've maligned the pope, what do you do next? Of course, send out another image of yourself as Jesus, curing the sick (though I swear, when I first saw it, I assumed the man being cured was Jeffrey Epstein — there is a resemblance — and that it must have been created by some satirist. That Trump sent it himself suggests a lack of self-awareness both magnificent and terrifying.</p><p>Called on it, Trump yanked down the image. The lie he floated... that he was a doctor, yeah, curing the sick; that's it! Because that's Donald Trump: he's all about healing. Then lickspittle Vice President JD Vance, fresh from sealing Viktor Orban's doom with his poisoned kiss, put the lie to that, claiming it was a joke that we are all too dense to understand.</p><p>Yeah, buddy, whatever.</p><p>For a number of years I've been out of the hope game. If the question is, will a feud with the pope reduce the Catholic support that buoyed Trump up, I'd trot out my standard line: once you get in the habit of ignoring reality, the exact nature of the reality being ignored hardly matters. If the Jan. 6 insurrection didn't trouble you, why should this?</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2026/04/13/pope-leo-president-trump-attack-iran-war-chicago-catholics-reaction" >Pope Leo brushes off Trump attack as a Chicago Catholic leader says president is ‘waking up a sleeping giant’</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>And Trump does have an astounding record of slurring popular figures and coming off golden, from his days mocking John McCain and American POWs ("I prefer people who weren't captured.") to his tirade against Taylor Swift ("I HATE TAYLOR SWIFT.")</p><p>Hard to believe, but the pope is even more popular than Taylor Swift.</p><p>So I'd never suggest that pushback is futile. Not after Minneapolis, when a bunch of bleeding heart liberals took the street and put down their lives for American democracy in a way that swaggering NRA types, marching into 7-Eleven with their assault rifles, would never dream of doing.</p><p>The good news is that finally, finally people are starting to notice. A news story on the front page of the New York Times Tuesday began, "President Trump's erratic behavior and extreme comments in recent days and weeks... have left many with the impression of a deranged autocrat mad with power."</p><p>Ya think? Not news to me. But still good to hear people finally say it.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Graduados de CPS obtienen sellos de biliteracidad en números récord, generando orgullo y abriendo puertas para muchos
entries.summary: <p>Cuando Anyeli Miranda y su familia llegaron a Chicago desde Guatemala hace aproximadamente siete años, se sintió como un pez fuera del agua.</p><p>Gran parte de eso se debía a la barrera del idioma que enfrentaba como hablante nativa de español. En su país de origen, tomó algunas clases de inglés, pero solo conocía frases básicas, como la de presentarse. Eso hacía que fuera un desafío conectar con sus compañeros cuando comenzó el sexto grado en Chicago.</p><p>“Al principio, fue un poco difícil ajustarse, hacer amigos, encontrar personas que te dejen ser parte de su grupo”, dijo Anyeli.</p><p>Ahora, como estudiante de último año en la Secundaria Roosevelt en Albany Park, Anyeli recuerda esos años con orgullo por lo lejos que ha llegado. Se está preparando para ir a la universidad el próximo año a estudiar enfermería y ha demostrado sus habilidades tanto en español como en inglés, que ya no ve como un obstáculo.</p><p>Aunque aún no es oficial, el diploma de graduación de Anyeli llevará un sello de biliteracidad, convirtiéndola en una de miles de estudiantes de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) que han obtenido un premio estatal que reconoce a los estudiantes que demuestran que pueden leer, escribir y hablar a un alto nivel en inglés y otro idioma.</p><p></p><p>El año pasado, más de 3,500 estudiantes de CPS obtuvieron el sello, un nuevo récord. Eso fue mucho más alto que en 2015, el primer año en que se presentaron premios a los graduados, cuando 91 estudiantes recibieron el reconocimiento. El número total de estudiantes de Illinois que obtuvieron el sello el año pasado también alcanzó un nivel histórico.</p><p>Educadores y expertos afirman que este crecimiento se debe a una combinación de factores, incluida una mayor conciencia del programa entre las familias, la expansión de las pruebas de idioma y un creciente interés en el multilingüismo, que puede hacer que los estudiantes sean más atractivos a la hora de postularse a universidades o empleos.</p><p>También puede ser una ventaja financiera. Las universidades públicas en Illinois otorgan créditos universitarios a los estudiantes que tienen el sello, ahorrándoles tiempo y dinero mientras trabajan para obtener sus títulos. Y los estudiantes dicen que el premio les hace sentirse orgullosos de sí mismos y de sus habilidades.</p><p>“Creo que es importante tenerlo porque es una buena oportunidad que abrirá puertas”, dijo Anyeli.</p><h3>Por qué las escuelas secundarias están otorgando más sellos de biliteracidad</h3><p>En 2013, Illinois se convirtió en el tercer estado en establecer un sello estatal de biliteracidad. Los primeros premios se presentaron a los graduados en 2015, cuando aproximadamente 500 estudiantes obtuvieron un sello en 10 idiomas diferentes. En la última década, el número de reconocimientos ha crecido. El año pasado, casi 11,000 estudiantes en Illinois obtuvieron el sello en 62 idiomas diferentes.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-60ef51" name="image-60ef51"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
